title: Reply to Comment on "Controlling the Dynamical Evolution of Quantum Coherence and Quantum Correlations in $e^{+}e^{-} \to Λ\barΛ$ Processes at BESIII"
abstract: We thank the Commentator for his detailed critique, which provides an opportunity to clarify the physical foundations of our work. While we appreciate the emphasis on rigor when applying quantum information concepts to high-energy physics (HEP), we respectfully disagree with the assertion that our assumptions lack a physical or operational basis. In the following sections, we address each point raised, demonstrating that our modeling is grounded in the established physics of QCD fragmentation and production dynamics in $e^+e^-\to Λ\barΛ$, as supported by recent experimental and theoretical advancements. Our approach treats the system as an effective open bipartite system during the intrinsic production stage, rather than during post-production propagation, which aligns with the analyses cited in our references.
summary: This paper is a formal rebuttal defending the use of quantum information metrics to study spin correlations in particle physics. The authors argue that the complex dynamics of QCD fragmentation can be validly modeled as an internal environment affecting the entanglement and steering of produced baryon pairs.
main_problem: The authors are rebutting a critique regarding the physical validity of using quantum information concepts, such as decoherence and quantum steering, to describe particle production in high-energy physics.
main_result: The authors defend their model by demonstrating that QCD fragmentation and hadronization can be treated as an effective internal environment that influences spin correlations.
method: The authors use quantum information theory tools, including quantum steering, entanglement, and discord, applied to reconstructed spin density matrices from angular distributions.
model_or_system: The system is the production of Lambda-antiLambda (Lambda-bar) baryon pairs in electron-positron annihilation processes at the BESIII experiment, modeled as an effective open bipartite system.
key_observables: Spin-spin correlations, phi angular distributions, and violations of steering inequalities.
important_parameters: Timelike electromagnetic form factors (EMFFs) and the distinction between Markovian and non-Markovian regimes.
main_assumptions: The model assumes an effective description where the QCD fragmentation phase acts as a dynamic internal environment rather than an external post-production bath.
paper_structure: The paper is structured as a point-by-point rebuttal, addressing the physical basis of the environment, the validity of steering measurements, and the rejection of operational measurement constraints.
why_interesting: It explores the application of open quantum system concepts (decoherence and non-Markovianity) to a high-energy particle production process, treating particle interactions as an internal environment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
